sections.title: Place
rows: There are amazing waterfalls **in** the rainforest.
Your passport is **in** the top drawer.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: in a minute / an hour / a year

sections.title: Be careful
items: With movement verbs (go, come, walk, run, move, fly), we often use **to** for destination: *go to Japan*.

sections.title: Place
rows: She left her glasses **on** the coffee table.
There's a map of the world **on** the wall.

sections.title: Be careful
items: **General vs. Specific**: We say *in the morning/afternoon/evening*, but we use **on** when a specific day is mentioned (**on Monday morning** / **on Wednesday evening** / etc.).
✓ We're flying to Washington in the morning.
✓ We're flying to Washington on Tuesday morning.
**No Preposition**: Do not use a preposition with *tomorrow*, *yesterday*, *tomorrow morning*, *yesterday evening*, etc.
✓ We're flying to Washington tomorrow afternoon.

sections.title: Time
rows: The film starts **at** quarter past eight.
Are you visiting your family **at** Easter?

sections.title: Place
rows: It gets very cold **at** the top of the mountain.
They used to live **at** 7 Park Avenue.
She must be **at** the gym — she said she was going this afternoon.
He couldn't come to the phone. He was **at** a meeting.

sections.title: Be careful
items: It is important to compare how we use **in** and **at** for places:
**In**: Used for larger areas that are all around us when we are there. Example: *We spent our holiday in the mountains.*
**At**: Used for smaller places and specific points on a journey. Example: *Can you meet me at the bus stop?*

exercises.title: A: Correct the preposition
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one wrong preposition. Write the correct one.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one wrong preposition. Write the correct one.

items.sentence: My sister was born at 2012.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "at" — years use in, not at, so the correction is "in".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use in with years, months, and longer periods — at is for precise times and places.

wrongOptions.at: "at" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "in".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "at" — the rule here needs "in" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions follow strict preposition rules: in for years/months, on for days, at for clock times.

items.sentence: The meeting is in Friday afternoon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "in" — parts of a day use on, so the correction is "on".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Days and parts of a day take on — in is for months, years, and longer periods.

wrongOptions.in: "in" is the highlighted error — use "on" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "in", not elsewhere — replace it with "on" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: The concert starts in half past eight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "in" — clock times use at, so the correction is "at".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Clock times use at — in is not used for exact times.

wrongOptions.in: "in" is the highlighted error — use "at" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "in", not elsewhere — replace it with "at" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions follow strict preposition rules: in for years/months, on for days, at for clock times.

items.sentence: We usually visit our cousins on August.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "on" — months use in, not on, so the correction is "in".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Months and years take in — on is for days and dates.

wrongOptions.on: "on" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "in".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "on" — the rule here needs "in" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: There were hundreds of fans ...... the stadium.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

exercises.title: E: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct preposition in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct preposition in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: I'll see you **at / on** the station in ten minutes.
items.completedSentence: I'll see you at the station in ten minutes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A specific point or place takes at.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: On is used with days and dates. At is used with specific times and places.

wrongOptions.at: At is not used with days or dates in standard English.
wrongOptions.on: On is not used for a precise meeting point like a station.

items.originalSentence: There is a fountain **at / in** front of the museum.
items.completedSentence: There is a fountain in front of the museum.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: This is a fixed expression or enclosed space that takes in.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: At marks a point (at the station). In marks inside or fixed phrases (in front of).

wrongOptions.at: At does not fit this type of location or fixed phrase.
wrongOptions.in: In does not fit when we need a point or a different preposition pattern.

items.originalSentence: The course begins **in / on** the third of September.
items.completedSentence: The course begins on the third of September.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The time expression in this sentence follows a fixed preposition pattern.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: In is used with months, years, and longer periods. On is used with days and dates.

wrongOptions.in: In does not match this type of time expression.
wrongOptions.on: On does not match this type of time expression.

items.originalSentence: We're visiting my aunt **in / on** Saturday morning.
items.completedSentence: We're visiting my aunt on Saturday morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The time expression in this sentence follows a fixed preposition pattern.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: In is used with months, years, and longer periods. On is used with days and dates.

wrongOptions.in: In does not match this type of time expression.
wrongOptions.on: On does not match this type of time expression.

items.originalSentence: Is there any milk **at / in** the fridge?
items.completedSentence: Is there any milk in the fridge?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: This is a fixed expression or enclosed space that takes in.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: At marks a point (at the station). In marks inside or fixed phrases (in front of).

wrongOptions.at: At does not fit this type of location or fixed phrase.
wrongOptions.in: In does not fit when we need a point or a different preposition pattern.

items.completedSentence: People may live on other planets in the future.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: This is a fixed expression or enclosed space that takes in.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: At marks a point (at the station). In marks inside or fixed phrases (in front of).

wrongOptions.at: At does not fit this type of location or fixed phrase.
wrongOptions.in: In does not fit when we need a point or a different preposition pattern.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A specific point or place takes at.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: On is used with days and dates. At is used with specific times and places.

wrongOptions.at: At is not used with days or dates in standard English.
wrongOptions.on: On is not used for a precise meeting point like a station.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: Her birthday is in 22nd April.

exercises.title: H: Sentence transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning to the first. Use a maximum of four words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning to the first. Use a maximum of four words.

items.promptSentence: The show starts at eight o'clock in the evening.
items.targetSentence: The show doesn't start ...... the morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Use in with parts of the day (morning, afternoon, evening).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use "" exactly as given and change the grammar around it to preserve the meaning of the first sentence.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 1 and 4 words, including the keyword "" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (1–4) before you check — include "" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

exercises.title: J: Mixed revision
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with one preposition.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with one preposition.

items.sentence: We met ...... the library after school.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a meeting point, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I keep my phone ...... my school bag.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inside a container, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: It gets very hot here ...... the summer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is seasons, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: We're going to a concert ...... Friday night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is day + time of day, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: There is a small café ...... the corner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: They live ...... 42 River Road.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is addresses, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We are travelling ...... the coast tomorrow.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is direction towards, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I found your wallet ...... the drawer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inside a container, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I'll meet you ...... the bus station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a specific place, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: She'll be here ...... a few seconds.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a period from now, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
